title: Find largest prime factor
description: Given N, find its largest prime factor

examples.explanation:
Step 1: N=315, sqrt(315)≈17.75. Initialize largest=1.
Step 2: i=2, 315%2≠0, skip.
Step 3: i=3, 315%3=0, check is_prime(3)=true, largest=3. Divide N: 315/3=105.
Step 4: Continue with i=3: 105%3=0, largest remains 3. Divide N: 105/3=35.
Step 5: i=3: 35%3≠0, move to i=4.
Step 6: i=4, 35%4≠0, skip.
Step 7: i=5, 35%5=0, check is_prime(5)=true, largest=5. Divide N: 35/5=7.
Step 8: i=5: 7%5≠0, move to i=6.
Step 9: i=6, 7%6≠0, skip.
Step 10: i=7, 7%7=0, check is_prime(7)=true, largest=7. Divide N: 7/7=1.
Step 11: Loop ends as i>sqrt(original N). N=1≤1, so no further check.
Step 12: Return largest=7.

Step 1: N=17, sqrt(17)≈4.12. Initialize largest=1.
Step 2: i=2, 17%2≠0, skip.
Step 3: i=3, 17%3≠0, skip.
Step 4: i=4, 17%4≠0, skip.
Step 5: Loop ends. N=17>1, check is_prime(17)=true, largest=max(1,17)=17.
Step 6: Return largest=17.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Iterate from 2 to sqrt(N), checking divisibility and primality of each factor. Update the largest prime factor found by dividing N by factors as they are discovered.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(sqrt(N) * sqrt(N)) = O(N) in worst case due to primality checks, but typically O(sqrt(N)) with optimizations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Largest Prime Factor Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the largest prime factor of N using sqrt(N) iteration with primality checks.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the largest prime factor of N by iterating through potential factors from 2 to sqrt(N). The intuition is that any composite number N can be expressed as a product of prime factors, and the largest prime factor will either be found among factors up to sqrt(N) or be N itself if N is prime. We check each integer i from 2 to sqrt(N) to see if it divides N. If i divides N, we check if i is prime. If i is prime, we update our largest prime factor candidate. We then divide N by i repeatedly to remove all factors of i from N. After the loop, if N is greater than 1, it means the remaining N is prime and could be the largest prime factor. The time complexity is O(sqrt(N) * sqrt(N)) = O(N) in worst case due to primality checks, but typically O(sqrt(N)) with optimizations like only checking odd numbers after 2. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the primality check subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling N=1 properly, not updating N after division, and inefficient primality checks. Real code pattern: Initialize largest = 1. For i from 2 to sqrt(N): while N % i == 0: if is_prime(i): largest = max(largest, i). N = N // i. If N > 1 and is_prime(N): largest = max(largest, N). Return largest.

examples.trace:
Step 1: N=315, sqrt(315)≈17.75. Initialize largest=1.
Step 2: i=2, 315%2≠0, skip.
Step 3: i=3, 315%3=0, check is_prime(3)=true, largest=3. Divide N: 315/3=105.
Step 4: Continue with i=3: 105%3=0, largest remains 3. Divide N: 105/3=35.
Step 5: i=3: 35%3≠0, move to i=4.
Step 6: i=4, 35%4≠0, skip.
Step 7: i=5, 35%5=0, check is_prime(5)=true, largest=5. Divide N: 35/5=7.
Step 8: i=5: 7%5≠0, move to i=6.
Step 9: i=6, 7%6≠0, skip.
Step 10: i=7, 7%7=0, check is_prime(7)=true, largest=7. Divide N: 7/7=1.
Step 11: Loop ends as i>sqrt(original N). N=1≤1, so no further check.
Step 12: Return largest=7.

Step 1: N=17, sqrt(17)≈4.12. Initialize largest=1.
Step 2: i=2, 17%2≠0, skip.
Step 3: i=3, 17%3≠0, skip.
Step 4: i=4, 17%4≠0, skip.
Step 5: Loop ends. N=17>1, check is_prime(17)=true, largest=max(1,17)=17.
Step 6: Return largest=17.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Primality Check Function
ladders.desc: Implement a function to check if a number is prime using trial division up to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a given integer n is prime by testing divisibility from 2 to sqrt(n). The intuition is that if n has any divisor other than 1 and itself, at least one divisor must be less than or equal to sqrt(n). We iterate i from 2 to sqrt(n). If n is divisible by any i, it's composite; otherwise, it's prime. Special cases: n <= 1 is not prime. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each factor candidate. Common mistakes include not handling n<=1, checking up to n instead of sqrt(n), and inefficiency with even numbers. Real code pattern: If n <= 1: return false. For i from 2 to sqrt(n): if n % i == 0: return false. Return true.

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=7>1, proceed. sqrt(7)≈2.65.
Step 2: i=2, 7%2=1≠0, continue.
Step 3: i=3>2.65, loop ends.
Step 4: No divisors found, return true.

Step 1: n=9>1, proceed. sqrt(9)=3.
Step 2: i=2, 9%2=1≠0, continue.
Step 3: i=3, 9%3=0, divisor found, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Factor Extraction with sqrt(N) Bound
ladders.desc: Extract all factors of N by iterating up to sqrt(N) and dividing N when a factor is found.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves finding factors of N by testing integers from 2 to sqrt(N). The intuition is that if N has a factor f, then N/f is also a factor, and at least one of f or N/f is ≤ sqrt(N). We iterate i from 2 to sqrt(N). If i divides N, we record i as a factor and update N by dividing by i repeatedly to remove all occurrences of i. This reduces N and avoids redundant checks. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor greater than sqrt(original N). Time complexity O(sqrt(N)), space O(1). This connects to L1 by providing factors to check for primality. Common mistakes include not using while loop for repeated division, not updating sqrt bound after division, and incorrect handling of large primes. Real code pattern: For i from 2 to sqrt(N): while N % i == 0: process factor i, N = N // i.

examples.trace:
Step 1: N=28, sqrt(28)≈5.29.
Step 2: i=2, 28%2=0: factor=2, N=28/2=14.
Step 3: i=2, 14%2=0: factor=2, N=14/2=7.
Step 4: i=2, 7%2≠0, move to i=3.
Step 5: i=3, 7%3≠0, move to i=4.
Step 6: i=4, 7%4≠0, move to i=5.
Step 7: i=5, 7%5≠0, loop ends.
Step 8: N=7>1, factor=7.
Step 9: Factors collected: 2,2,7.

Step 1: N=17, sqrt(17)≈4.12.
Step 2: i=2, 17%2≠0, skip.
Step 3: i=3, 17%3≠0, skip.
Step 4: i=4, 17%4≠0, skip.
Step 5: Loop ends.
Step 6: N=17>1, factor=17.
Step 7: Factors: 17.

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Division and Modulo Operations
ladders.desc: Perform division and modulo operations to check divisibility and update numbers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using division (/) and modulo (%) operations to manipulate integers. The intuition is that a % b gives the remainder when a is divided by b; if a % b == 0, then b divides a. Division a // b gives the quotient, used to reduce a after removing factor b. These operations are fundamental for testing factors and updating N during factorization. Time and space complexity are O(1) per operation. This connects to L2 by enabling factor extraction. Common mistakes include using floating-point division instead of integer division, not handling division by zero, and misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers. Real code pattern: Use % to check divisibility: if N % i == 0. Use // to update: N = N // i.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute 15 % 3: 15 divided by 3 is 5 remainder 0, so 15 % 3 = 0.
Step 2: Compute 15 // 3: integer division gives quotient 5, so 15 // 3 = 5.
Step 3: Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 15, and new value is 5.

Step 1: Compute 17 % 4: 17 divided by 4 is 4 remainder 1, so 17 % 4 = 1.
Step 2: Compute 17 // 4: integer division gives quotient 4, so 17 // 4 = 4.
Step 3: Since remainder is 1, 4 does not divide 17.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping from 2 to sqrt(N)
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that iterates through integers from 2 to the square root of N.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating a loop that runs from i=2 to i<=sqrt(N). The intuition is that to check factors or primality, we only need to test up to sqrt(N) because any factor larger than sqrt(N) would pair with a smaller factor. We compute sqrt(N) once to avoid recomputation. Use integer math for efficiency. Time complexity O(sqrt(N)) for the loop, space O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the iteration structure for division checks. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt causing precision issues, not converting to integer, or looping up to N which is inefficient. Real code pattern: limit = int(sqrt(N)). For i in range(2, limit+1): perform operations.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute sqrt(16)=4.
Step 2: Convert to integer: limit=4.
Step 3: Loop i=2,3,4.
Step 4: For each i, perform desired operation (e.g., check if i divides N).

Step 1: Compute sqrt(10)≈3.16.
Step 2: Convert to integer: limit=3.
Step 3: Loop i=2,3.
Step 4: Operate for each i.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Factors and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Learn what it means for one number to divide another and identify factors.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers factors and divisibility. A factor (or divisor) of a number N is an integer that divides N without leaving a remainder. For example, factors of 6 are 1,2,3,6 because 6/1=6, 6/2=3, etc., all with remainder 0. Divisibility means N % d == 0. Every integer N has at least factors 1 and N. Prime numbers have exactly two factors: 1 and themselves. Composite numbers have more than two factors. The intuition is that finding factors helps break down numbers into simpler parts. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 by providing the basis for loop checks. Common mistakes include confusing factors with multiples, or not considering 1 and N as factors. Real code pattern: To check if d is a factor of N: if N % d == 0.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check d=1: 12 % 1 = 0, so 1 is a factor.
Step 2: d=2: 12 % 2 = 0, factor.
Step 3: d=3: 12 % 3 = 0, factor.
Step 4: d=4: 12 % 4 = 0, factor.
Step 5: d=5: 12 % 5 = 2 ≠0, not factor.
Step 6: d=6: 12 % 6 = 0, factor.
Step 7: d=7 to 11: remainders ≠0.
Step 8: d=12: 12 % 12 = 0, factor.
Step 9: Factors are 1,2,3,4,6,12.

Step 1: Check d=1: 7 % 1 = 0, factor.
Step 2: d=2: 7 % 2 = 1 ≠0, not factor.
Step 3: d=3: 7 % 3 = 1 ≠0.
Step 4: d=4: 7 % 4 = 3 ≠0.
Step 5: d=5: 7 % 5 = 2 ≠0.
Step 6: d=6: 7 % 6 = 1 ≠0.
Step 7: d=7: 7 % 7 = 0, factor.
Step 8: Factors are 1 and 7, so 7 is prime.

approaches.name: Factorization Reduction O(sqrt(N))
approaches.summary: Divide N by all factors starting from 2, reducing N each time a factor is found, and track the largest factor. This eliminates the need for explicit primality checks by ensuring only prime factors are considered.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Largest Prime Factor via Factorization Reduction
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding the largest prime factor of N using the factorization reduction approach.
ladders.explanation:
This approach efficiently finds the largest prime factor by systematically dividing N by all possible factors starting from 2, reducing N each time a factor is found, and tracking the largest factor. The key insight is that by dividing N by each factor completely (using a while loop), we ensure that only prime factors are considered without needing explicit primality checks. When we encounter a factor i, we repeatedly divide N by i until it's no longer divisible, which removes all occurrences of i from N. This means that any composite factors will have already been broken down into their prime components earlier in the process. For example, if N has a factor 4, it will be handled when i=2, since 4=2×2. After processing all factors up to sqrt(N), if N remains greater than 1, it must be a prime number itself (the largest prime factor). The algorithm runs in O(sqrt(N)) time because we only check factors up to sqrt(N), and each division operation reduces N significantly. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach is optimal for this problem because it avoids the overhead of primality testing and leverages mathematical properties of factorization.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize largest_factor = 1
2. While N is divisible by 2:
   - Set largest_factor = 2
   - Divide N by 2
3. For i from 3 to sqrt(N), step by 2:
   - While N is divisible by i:
     - Set largest_factor = i
     - Divide N by i
4. If N > 1, set largest_factor = N
5. Return largest_factor

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the core factorization reduction logic from L1 as its main driver, wrapped with initialization and final checks.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to handle the factor 2 separately before the odd-number loop
- Not using integer division when reducing N
- Stopping the loop at i <= N instead of i <= sqrt(N)
- Not checking if N > 1 at the end to capture the final prime factor

Real code pattern:
def largest_prime_factor(N):
    largest = 1
    # Handle factor 2
    while N % 2 == 0:
        largest = 2
        N //= 2
    # Handle odd factors
    i = 3
    while i * i <= N:
        while N % i == 0:
            largest = i
            N //= i
        i += 2
    # If N is still > 1, it's prime
    if N > 1:
        largest = N
    return largest

examples.trace:
N=315
Step 1: Handle factor 2: 315%2=1 (not divisible), largest=1, N=315
Step 2: i=3, 3*3=9 <= 315
   - 315%3=0, so divisible
   - largest=3, N=315//3=105
   - 105%3=0, largest=3, N=105//3=35
   - 35%3=2 (not divisible), exit inner while
Step 3: i=5, 5*5=25 <= 35
   - 35%5=0, largest=5, N=35//5=7
   - 7%5=2 (not divisible), exit inner while
Step 4: i=7, 7*7=49 > 7, exit outer while
Step 5: N=7 > 1, so largest=7
Return: 7

N=13195
Step 1: Handle factor 2: 13195%2=1 (not divisible), largest=1, N=13195
Step 2: i=3, 3*3=9 <= 13195
   - 13195%3=1 (not divisible)
Step 3: i=5, 5*5=25 <= 13195
   - 13195%5=0, largest=5, N=13195//5=2639
   - 2639%5=4 (not divisible), exit inner while
Step 4: i=7, 7*7=49 <= 2639
   - 2639%7=0, largest=7, N=2639//7=377
   - 377%7=6 (not divisible), exit inner while
Step 5: i=9 (skip as not prime, but algorithm checks anyway): 377%9=8 (not divisible)
Step 6: i=11, 11*11=121 <= 377
   - 377%11=3 (not divisible)
Step 7: i=13, 13*13=169 <= 377
   - 377%13=0, largest=13, N=377//13=29
   - 29%13=3 (not divisible), exit inner while
Step 8: i=15 (skip): 29%15=14 (not divisible)
Step 9: i=17, 17*17=289 > 29, exit outer while
Step 10: N=29 > 1, so largest=29
Return: 29

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Factorization Reduction Loop
ladders.desc: The core loop that reduces N by dividing by factors and tracks the largest factor.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine implements the heart of the factorization reduction approach. The intuition is that by iteratively dividing N by each potential factor i (starting from 2, then odd numbers), we can reduce N while simultaneously identifying prime factors. When we find that N is divisible by i, we know i is a factor. By using a while loop to divide N by i repeatedly until it's no longer divisible, we ensure that all occurrences of i are removed from N. This is crucial because it means that any composite multiples of i (like i×i, i×i×i, etc.) won't be checked later, as N no longer contains them. For example, if N=12 and i=2, we divide by 2 twice to get N=3, so we never need to check i=4 or i=6. The loop continues while i×i ≤ N because if N has a factor greater than sqrt(N), the corresponding co-factor must be less than sqrt(N), and would have been found earlier. After the loop, if N>1, it's a prime number that wasn't divided out (because it's greater than sqrt(N) of the original N). This sub-routine runs in O(sqrt(N)) time in the worst case (when N is prime or a product of two large primes), but often faster as N gets reduced. Space is O(1).

Connection to L0: This is the main algorithmic core that L0 wraps with initialization and finalization.

Common mistakes:
- Not using while N%i==0 to remove all occurrences of a factor
- Incrementing i inside the inner while loop
- Using floating point sqrt instead of i*i <= N

Real code pattern:
def factorize_reduce(N):
    largest = 1
    # Handle 2 separately
    while N % 2 == 0:
        largest = 2
        N //= 2
    i = 3
    while i * i <= N:
        while N % i == 0:
            largest = i
            N //= i
        i += 2
    if N > 1:
        largest = N
    return largest

examples.trace:
N=18
Step 1: Handle 2: 18%2=0, largest=2, N=9
         18%2=0 again? Wait, N is now 9, so re-check: 9%2=1, exit while
Step 2: i=3, 3*3=9 <= 9
   - 9%3=0, largest=3, N=3
   - 3%3=0, largest=3, N=1
   - 1%3=1, exit inner while
Step 3: i=5, 5*5=25 > 1, exit outer while
Step 4: N=1, not >1, so keep largest=3
Return: 3

N=105
Step 1: Handle 2: 105%2=1, not divisible, largest=1, N=105
Step 2: i=3, 3*3=9 <= 105
   - 105%3=0, largest=3, N=35
   - 35%3=2, exit inner while
Step 3: i=5, 5*5=25 <= 35
   - 35%5=0, largest=5, N=7
   - 7%5=2, exit inner while
Step 4: i=7, 7*7=49 > 7, exit outer while
Step 5: N=7 > 1, so largest=7
Return: 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Eliminating Composite Factors via Complete Division
ladders.desc: The technique of repeatedly dividing N by a factor until it's no longer divisible to ensure only prime factors are considered.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic is the mathematical insight that makes the factorization reduction approach work without explicit primality checks. The intuition is based on the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely represented as a product of prime numbers. When we find that N is divisible by a number i, we don't just record i as a factor and move on—instead, we repeatedly divide N by i until N is no longer divisible by i. This ensures that all occurrences of i are removed from N. Why does this guarantee we only deal with prime factors? Consider a composite number like 6. If N has a factor 6, it must also have factors 2 and 3 (since 6=2×3). When our algorithm reaches i=2, it will divide out all factors of 2 from N. Then when it reaches i=3, it will divide out all factors of 3. By the time we reach i=6, N will no longer be divisible by 6 because all 2's and 3's have been removed. Therefore, the while N%i==0 loop naturally filters out composite factors, leaving us only with prime factors. This works because we check factors in increasing order (2, 3, 4, 5... though 4 gets skipped as explained). The time complexity for this operation is O(k) where k is the exponent of the prime factor in N's factorization. Space is O(1).

Connection to L1: This is the key mechanism inside the factorization reduction loop.

Common mistakes:
- Using if instead of while for the divisibility check
- Not updating N after each division
- Not understanding why this eliminates composite factors

Real code pattern:
while N % i == 0:
    # i is a factor
    N = N // i
# Continue with next i

examples.trace:
Start: N=12, i=2
Step 1: Check 12%2==0? Yes
   - Divide: N=12//2=6
Step 2: Check 6%2==0? Yes
   - Divide: N=6//2=3
Step 3: Check 3%2==0? No, exit while loop
End: N=3, i=2 removed completely

Start: N=125, i=5
Step 1: Check 125%5==0? Yes
   - Divide: N=125//5=25
Step 2: Check 25%5==0? Yes
   - Divide: N=25//5=5
Step 3: Check 5%5==0? Yes
   - Divide: N=5//5=1
Step 4: Check 1%5==0? No, exit while loop
End: N=1, all factors of 5 removed

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Determining if one number divides another and performing integer division.
ladders.explanation:
This building block covers two fundamental operations: checking divisibility (using the modulo operator %) and performing integer division (using // in Python or / with floor in other languages). Divisibility is the property that integer A is divisible by integer B if A % B == 0, meaning B divides A without leaving a remainder. For example, 10 % 2 == 0, so 10 is divisible by 2. Integer division gives the quotient when dividing two integers, discarding any remainder. For example, 10 // 3 == 3 (since 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1). In the factorization reduction algorithm, we use these operations together: first check if N % i == 0 to see if i is a factor, then if it is, update N = N // i to remove that factor. The intuition is that if i divides N, then N can be expressed as i × (N//i), so dividing N by i reduces it while preserving the factorization structure. It's crucial to use integer division (not float division) to keep N as an integer. The time complexity of these operations is O(1) for typical integer sizes. Space is O(1).

Connection to L2: These operations are used repeatedly in the while N%i==0 loop to test and remove factors.

Common mistakes:
- Using / instead of // for integer division in Python
- Not handling division by zero
- Confusing % (modulo) with / (division)

Real code pattern:
if N % i == 0:
    N = N // i

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check divisibility: 15 % 3 = 0, so True
Step 2: Perform integer division: 15 // 3 = 5
Result: N becomes 5

Step 1: Check divisibility: 17 % 4 = 1, not 0, so False
Step 2: No division performed
Result: N remains 17

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division Operators
ladders.desc: Understanding and applying the % (modulo) and // (integer division) operators.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the basic mathematical operators that are essential for the factorization algorithm. The modulo operator (%) returns the remainder when dividing the left operand by the right operand. For example, 10 % 3 = 1 because 10 ÷ 3 = 3 with remainder 1. The integer division operator (//) returns the quotient, discarding any remainder. For example, 10 // 3 = 3. In programming, these operators work on integers and are typically O(1) operations. The modulo operator is particularly useful for checking divisibility: if a % b == 0, then a is divisible by b. This is because divisible means the remainder is zero. Integer division is used to reduce a number when we know it's divisible by another. For instance, if we know 15 is divisible by 3 (because 15 % 3 == 0), then 15 // 3 = 5 gives us the result after removing one factor of 3. It's important to note that // always rounds down to the nearest integer (floor division), which is what we want for integer factorization. These operators are fundamental building blocks for many algorithms beyond just factorization.

Connection to L3: These operators are the tools used to implement divisibility checking and integer division.

Common mistakes:
- Using / instead of // in Python 3
- Expecting % to give the quotient instead of remainder
- Not handling negative numbers (though not needed here)

Real code pattern:
remainder = a % b
quotient = a // b

examples.trace:
Step 1: Modulo: 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2, so 17 % 5 = 2
Step 2: Integer division: 17 ÷ 5 = 3.4, floor to 3, so 17 // 5 = 3

Step 1: Modulo: 24 ÷ 6 = 4 remainder 0, so 24 % 6 = 0
Step 2: Integer division: 24 ÷ 6 = 4 exactly, so 24 // 6 = 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Factors and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understanding what it means for one number to be a factor of another.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept covers factors and divisibility, which are prerequisites for understanding prime factorization. A factor (or divisor) of a number N is an integer that divides N exactly, i.e., without leaving a remainder. For example, factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12 because 12 ÷ 1 = 12, 12 ÷ 2 = 6, 12 ÷ 3 = 4, etc., all with remainder 0. Divisibility is the property that N is divisible by d if N % d == 0. Every integer N has at least two factors: 1 and N itself. Prime numbers have exactly two factors (1 and themselves), while composite numbers have more than two factors. The largest prime factor is the greatest prime number that divides N. For example, for N=12, the prime factors are 2 and 3 (since 12=2×2×3), and the largest is 3. Understanding factors is essential because our algorithm finds factors of N and then determines which are prime. This concept doesn't involve code yet—it's pure mathematical understanding. It's the basis for all subsequent levels.

Connection to L4: Divisibility (N % d == 0) is how we check if d is a factor of N.

Common mistakes:
- Confusing factors with multiples
- Thinking 1 is prime (it's not)
- Not understanding that every number is divisible by 1 and itself

Real code pattern:
# Conceptual understanding only at this level
# A factor d of N satisfies: N % d == 0

examples.output: Factors: 1,2,3,6; Prime factors: 2,3; Largest prime factor: 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: List all numbers that divide 6 without remainder:
   - 6÷1=6 remainder 0 → 1 is factor
   - 6÷2=3 remainder 0 → 2 is factor
   - 6÷3=2 remainder 0 → 3 is factor
   - 6÷4=1 remainder 2 → 4 is NOT factor
   - 6÷5=1 remainder 1 → 5 is NOT factor
   - 6÷6=1 remainder 0 → 6 is factor
Step 2: Identify prime factors (factors that are prime numbers):
   - 2 is prime (factors: 1,2)
   - 3 is prime (factors: 1,3)
Step 3: Largest prime factor: max(2,3)=3

examples.output: Factors: 1,13; Prime factors: 13; Largest prime factor: 13
examples.trace:
Step 1: List all numbers that divide 13 without remainder:
   - 13÷1=13 remainder 0 → 1 is factor
   - 13÷2=6 remainder 1 → 2 is NOT factor
   - 13÷3=4 remainder 1 → 3 is NOT factor
   ...
   - 13÷13=1 remainder 0 → 13 is factor
Step 2: Identify prime factors:
   - 13 is prime (only factors 1 and 13)
Step 3: Largest prime factor: 13

approaches.name: Pollard's Rho Algorithm O(N^(1/4))
approaches.summary: Use a probabilistic algorithm to find factors quickly by detecting cycles in a pseudo-random sequence. Combine with trial division for small factors and recursion to handle composite numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Largest Prime Factor Using Pollard's Rho Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation combining Pollard's Rho for factor detection, trial division for small factors, and recursion to handle composite numbers.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the largest prime factor of N by intelligently decomposing it. The intuition is that large numbers are hard to factor naively, but Pollard's Rho algorithm can detect non-trivial factors quickly by exploiting the birthday paradox in a pseudo-random sequence. We start with trial division up to N^(1/4) to remove small prime factors efficiently. For the remaining composite part, we use Pollard's Rho: generate a sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod N where f(x)=x^2+c (c random), and look for cycles using Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise-hare). When gcd(|x_i - x_j|, N) > 1, we've found a factor. Recursively apply this to both factors until all are prime. The largest prime emerges naturally. Time is O(N^(1/4) * polylog(N)) average due to Pollard's Rho's probabilistic nature and trial division cutoff. Space is O(1) aside from recursion stack. Connection: This integrates all lower-level components into a working solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle N=1, not randomizing c in Pollard's Rho (can get stuck), and missing recursion base cases. Real code pattern: def largest_prime_factor(N): if N==1: return 1; for p in primes up to N^(1/4): while N%p==0: N//=p; if is_prime(N): return N; d=pollard_rho(N); return max(largest_prime_factor(d), largest_prime_factor(N//d)).

examples.trace: Step 1: Trial division up to 315^(1/4)≈4.2, check 2,3. N=315, 315%3=0 → factor 3, N=105. 105%3=0 → factor 3, N=35. 35%3≠0. Check 2: 35%2≠0. Step 2: N=35, is_prime(35)? No. Pollard's Rho: set x=2, y=2, d=1, c=1. While d==1: x=(x*x+1)%35=5, y=(y*y+1)%35=26, y=(26*26+1)%35=22, d=gcd(|5-22|,35)=gcd(17,35)=1. Continue: x=26, y=... eventually x=15, y=15, d=gcd(0,35)=35 → no factor (needs retry with different c). Actually, simpler: 35=5*7, trial division caught 5? Wait, trial division only up to 4.2, so 5 not checked. Pollard's Rho with c=1: sequence cycles, find d=gcd(|x-y|,35)=7 at some point. So factor 7 and 5. Recursively: largest_prime_factor(7)=7, largest_prime_factor(5)=5. Max is 7.

Step 1: Trial division up to 13195^(1/4)≈10.7, check primes 2,3,5,7. N=13195, 13195%5=0 → factor 5, N=2639. 2639%5≠0. 2639%7=0? 7*377=2639, yes → factor 7, N=377. 377%7≠0. Step 2: N=377, is_prime(377)? 377=13*29, no. Pollard's Rho: with c=1, x=2,y=2,d=1. Iterate: x=5,y=26,d=gcd(21,377)=1; x=26,y=... eventually find d=gcd(|x-y|,377)=13. Factors 13 and 29. Recursively: largest_prime_factor(13)=13, largest_prime_factor(29)=29. Max is 29.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pollard's Rho Factor Detection
ladders.desc: Implement Pollard's Rho algorithm to find a non-trivial factor of a composite number N.
ladders.explanation: Pollard's Rho is a probabilistic algorithm that finds a factor of N by detecting a cycle in a pseudo-random sequence modulo N. The intuition: if we generate a sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod N where f is a polynomial (like x^2 + c), by the birthday paradox, two values in the sequence will collide modulo a factor p of N faster than modulo N itself. When x_i ≡ x_j (mod p) but x_i ≠ x_j (mod N), then gcd(|x_i - x_j|, N) will be a multiple of p, revealing a factor. We use Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise and hare) to find such a collision efficiently without storing the sequence. Algorithm: choose random x, y, c; set d=1; while d==1: x = f(x) mod N; y = f(f(y)) mod N; d = gcd(|x-y|, N); if d==N, retry with different c. Time: O(N^(1/4)) on average for finding a factor, as the cycle length is about √p. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the core factor-finding mechanism in the full solution. Common mistakes: not handling d==N (algorithm fails), using fixed c (may not find cycle), and infinite loops for prime N. Real code pattern: def pollard_rho(N): if N%2==0: return 2; x=random(2,N-1); y=x; c=random(1,N-1); d=1; while d==1: x=(x*x+c)%N; y=(y*y+c)%N; y=(y*y+c)%N; d=gcd(abs(x-y),N); if d==N: return pollard_rho(N) (retry). Return d.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=35, not even. Choose x=2, y=2, c=1. d=1. While d==1: x=(2*2+1)%35=5; y=(2*2+1)%35=5; y=(5*5+1)%35=26; d=gcd(|5-26|,35)=gcd(21,35)=7. d=7≠1, so factor 7 found. Alternatively, might find 5 with different c.
Step 1: N=91, not even. x=2,y=2,c=1. d=1. Iteration 1: x=5,y=26,d=gcd(21,91)=7. Factor 7 found. Check: 91=7*13.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cycle Detection via Floyd's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Use Floyd's tortoise and hare method to detect cycles in a sequence without extra memory.
ladders.explanation: Floyd's cycle detection algorithm efficiently finds a cycle in a sequence by using two pointers that move at different speeds. The intuition: if a sequence has a cycle, a fast pointer (hare) will eventually lap the slow pointer (tortoise), indicating a collision. For Pollard's Rho, we generate sequence x_i = f(x_{i-1}) mod N. We set tortoise x and hare y, both starting at the same value. In each step, tortoise moves one step: x = f(x), while hare moves two steps: y = f(f(y)). If x ≡ y (mod p) for some factor p of N, then gcd(|x-y|, N) may reveal p. This works because the sequence modulo p will cycle faster than modulo N. Algorithm: initialize x, y to seed; while True: x = f(x); y = f(f(y)); if gcd(|x-y|, N) > 1, return it as factor. Time: O(√p) steps on average to find collision, where p is the smallest factor. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the mechanism within Pollard's Rho to find factors. Common mistakes: not updating hare correctly (must apply f twice), and missing the gcd check each step. Real code pattern: while d==1: x=(x*x+c)%N; y=(y*y+c)%N; y=(y*y+c)%N; d=gcd(abs(x-y),N).

examples.trace: Step 1: x=1,y=1. x=2; y: f(1)=2, f(2)=5. |2-5|=3, gcd(3,21)=3. Factor 3 found quickly.

ladders.title: Building Block — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) via Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Compute gcd of two numbers using the efficient Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm finds the greatest common divisor (gcd) of two integers by repeatedly applying the property gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). The intuition: any common divisor of a and b also divides their difference, and by reducing the problem with modulo, we quickly converge to the gcd. Algorithm: while b ≠ 0: set temp = b; b = a mod b; a = temp; return a. For example, gcd(48,18): 48 mod 18 = 12, then gcd(18,12): 18 mod 12 = 6, then gcd(12,6): 12 mod 6 = 0, so gcd is 6. Time: O(log min(a,b)) as numbers reduce quickly. Space: O(1). Connection: In Pollard's Rho, gcd is used to check if |x-y| shares a non-trivial factor with N. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (use absolute values), and infinite loop if b=0 initially. Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: a=35, b=21. 35 mod 21 = 14, so a=21, b=14. Step 2: 21 mod 14 = 7, so a=14, b=7. Step 3: 14 mod 7 = 0, so a=7, b=0. Return 7.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic and Random Number Generation
ladders.desc: Perform modulo operations and generate pseudo-random numbers within a range.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic computes the remainder when dividing one number by another, written as a mod N = r where 0 ≤ r < N. The intuition: it wraps numbers around a cycle, essential for Pollard's Rho sequence generation. For example, 17 mod 5 = 2 because 17 = 3*5 + 2. In code, use the % operator. Random number generation selects unpredictable values; for Pollard's Rho, we need random starting x and constant c in range [1, N-1] to avoid fixed points and ensure sequence variety. Algorithm: use a PRNG like linear congruential or system rand, scaled to range. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection: These operations build the sequence x_i = (x_{i-1}^2 + c) mod N in Pollard's Rho. Common mistakes: generating c=0 (leads to trivial sequence), and not handling negative mod results (in some languages, % can be negative; use abs or adjust). Real code pattern: import random; x = random.randint(2, N-1); c = random.randint(1, N-1); next_x = (x*x + c) % N.

examples.trace: Step 1: 23 divided by 7 is 3 with remainder 2, since 3*7=21, 23-21=2. So 23 mod 7 = 2.
Step 1: Use a random function that returns integer between 1 and 10 inclusive. Example output might be 7. This is pseudo-random, so vary each run.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and how to check divisibility.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are prime. Divisibility means one number can be divided by another without leaving a remainder; a divides b if b % a == 0. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of integers, as any integer can be factored uniquely into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). To check if a number N is prime, test divisibility by integers from 2 to √N; if any divides N, it's composite. Algorithm: if N <= 1: return False; for i from 2 to √N: if N % i == 0: return False; return True. Time: O(√N). Space: O(1). Connection: This foundational concept is used in trial division and verifying prime factors in the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to check up to √N (checking up to N-1 is inefficient), and mishandling N=1 (not prime). Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: 7 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to √7≈2.6, so check 2: 7%2=1 ≠0. No other divisors, so 7 is prime.
Step 1: 9 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to √9=3. Check 2: 9%2=1 ≠0. Check 3: 9%3=0, so divisible, thus 9 is composite (not prime).
